## Overview

This practice is mostly **hands-on**. You will set up the real folder structure you will use for the rest of the course, create your R Project, write your first `.qmd` from scratch, and practice loading packages вЂ” all skills you will rely on every week.

Topics covered (Lessons 7вЂ“10):

- Folder and file organization
- Quarto and `.qmd` files
- Packages
- R Projects and working directories

## Directions

Practice assignments will follow a general format. Here are the following steps you need to follow to complete this assignment:

1. [Download the practice `.qmd` from my GitHub repository.](https://github.com/nwakim/PUBH_523_26Su/blob/main/practice/practice_02.qmd)
2. Save the `.qmd` file as `Lastname_Firstinitial_Practice_02.qmd` or `lastname_firstinitial_practice_02.qmd` (replace with your actual last name and first initial). Save it in your `practice/` folder with a `practice_02` subfolder if you'd like.
3. Open the `.qmd` in RStudio (within your project) and edit it to complete the assignment. You will add your answers directly into this file.
4. Keep the the boxes with the yellow stripe on the left side that explain the question. 
5. Do all your work under and outside of the boxes with the yellow stripe. 
6. You can delete other parts of this assignment, including:
    - Overview 
    - Directions
    - Boxes that contain tips
    - Submission checklist
    - Note on AI usage
7. Submit your completed practice to Brightspace as both a `.qmd` and `.html` file. 

## Questions

### Question 1: Set Up Your Course Folder

In our lesson on folder and file organization (L7), we learned that every project should live in **one well-organized folder**. In this question, you will create that folder and use it for the rest of the course.

#### Part A: Create the folder structure

::: {.callout-warning appearance="minimal"}
##### Part A Task

On your computer, create a folder called `PUBH_523_26Su` or `PUBH_623_26Su` somewhere that makes sense (your Documents folder, OneDrive, etc.). Inside it, create the following subfolders (feel free to change some of the subfolders if you have a preferred organization):

```         
PUBH_523_26Su/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ admin/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ data_lessons/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ data_practice/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ data_project/
в”‚   в”њв”Ђв”Ђ data_raw/
в”‚   в””в”Ђв”Ђ data_processed/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ lessons/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ practice/
в”њв”Ђв”Ђ project/
в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.txt
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
##### Part A Tips

- Create `README.txt` as a plain text file (open Notepad on Windows or TextEdit on Mac, save as `.txt`).
- Create `data_raw/` and `data_processed/` *inside* the `data_project/` folder.
- Folder names should be lowercase with underscores вЂ” no spaces!
- You can also copy the folders from our shared folder
:::

#### Part B: Take a screenshot and insert it here

::: {.callout-warning appearance="minimal"}
##### Part B Task
Take a screenshot of your `PUBH_523_26Su` folder open in your file browser (Finder on Mac, File Explorer on Windows) so that the subfolders are visible.
:::

::: {.callout-caution appearance="minimal"}
##### Part B Needed Steps

**New skill: inserting an image into a `.qmd`**

Quarto makes it easy to embed images using Markdown syntax. Here is how:

**Step 1.** Save your screenshot somewhere you can find it, ideally in your `practice/` folder where you create a `practice_02` subfolder. You can name something like `practice_02_folders.png`.

**Step 2.** In this `.qmd` file, insert the image.

- You can do this in the Visual editor by clicking the picture icon (![](./images_practice/practice_02_insert_image.png){width="30"}) up top and browsing for your file. If you do this, switch to Source editor to see how the picture insert translates to markdown.
- You can do this in the Source editor by typing the following line, replacing the path with your own: `![Caption describing your image](path/to/your/image.png)`
  - Note that `here()` does not work for image paths

**Step 3.** Render the document and confirm the screenshot appears in the HTML output.
:::

### Question 2: Packages

#### Part A: Load the course packages

::: {.callout-warning appearance="minimal"}
##### Task
In Lesson 9, we learned to use `pacman::p_load()` to install and load packages in one step. In the code chunk below, use `pacman::p_load()` to load **all of the following** packages:

`tidyverse`, `here`, `readxl`, `haven`, `writexl`, `janitor`, `rstatix`, `gt`, `gtsummary`, `naniar`
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Load all course packages here using pacman::p_load()
```
:::


::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
##### Tip
If `pacman` itself is not installed yet, run `install.packages("pacman")` in your Console first (just once, do not put `install.packages()` in your `.qmd`!).
:::

#### Part B: What does each package do?

Based on Lesson 9, fill in the table below. Each package belongs to one of three categories from the lesson: **loading/saving data**, **summarizing/visualizing data**, or **wrangling data**. Note one specific thing each package is used for.

| Package                       | Category | One thing it is used for |
|-------------------------------|----------|--------------------------|
| `here`                        |          |                          |
| `readxl`                      |          |                          |
| `haven`                       |          |                          |
| `janitor`                     |          |                          |
| `gtsummary`                   |          |                          |
| `dplyr` (part of tidyverse)   |          |                          |
| `ggplot2` (part of tidyverse) |          |                          |

::: callout-tip
##### Tip
You can use the `?` command in R to learn more about what a package does. You can also google each! 
:::

#### Part C: Install vs. load

A classmate wrote the following at the top of their `.qmd` file. Identify the problem and explain how to fix it:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
install.packages("tidyverse")
library(tidyverse)
```
:::


*What is wrong with putting `install.packages()` inside a `.qmd`, and what should they do instead?*

### Question 3: Your First Practice `.qmd`

In Lesson 8, we walked through the three components of a `.qmd` file: the YAML header, text, and code chunks. In this question, you will demonstrate that you can create, edit, and render a `.qmd` file correctly.

#### Part A: Downloading the practice file

You can download this practice from my GitHub repository [here](https://github.com/nwakim/PUBH_523_26Su/blob/main/practice/practice_02.qmd).

Save the file with the following name: `Lastname_Firstinitial_Practice_02.qmd` or `lastname_firstinitial_practice_02.qmd` (replace with your actual last name and first initial). Save it in your `practice/` folder with a `practice_02` subfolder if you'd like. 


#### Part A: Check your YAML header

Scroll to the very top of this file. Make sure the YAML header has:

- Your actual name in `author:`
- `date-modified: "today"` (so it updates automatically each time you render)

Once you have updated the author field, render the document. Confirm your name and today's date appear at the top of the HTML output.

#### Part B: Add a heading and paragraph

Directly below this sentence, add:

1.  A **level 3 heading** (`###`) with a title of your choice (something about yourself or why you are taking this course)
2.  A short paragraph (2вЂ“4 sentences) of text (can be jibberish) under that heading

You can use the Source editor or the Visual Editor.

*Add your heading and paragraph here:*

#### Part C: Code chunk options

Below is a code chunk that calculates the mean of a small vector. Adjust the chunk options so that:

- The **code is hidden** in the rendered output (results still show)
- **Warnings are suppressed**


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
x <- c(4, 8, 15, 16, 23, 42)
mean(x)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 18
```


:::
:::


::: callout-tip
##### Hint
Add the appropriate `#|` options at the top of the chunk.
:::

#### Part D: Render and confirm

Render this document to HTML. Confirm that:

- Your screenshot from Question 1 appears
- Your heading and paragraph from Part B appear
- The code chunk output from Part C shows the result but **not** the code

### Question 4: Create Your R Project

We learned that an R Project (`.Rproj` file) sets the working directory automatically so you never need `setwd()`.

#### Part A: Create the project

Create an R Project **inside your existing `PUBH_523_26Su` folder**:

1.  In RStudio: **File в†’ New Project в†’ Existing Directory**
2.  Browse to and select your `PUBH_523_26Su` or `PUBH_623_26Su` folder
3.  Click **Create Project**

After creating the project, your folder should now contain a `PUBH_523_26Su.Rproj` or `PUBH_623_26Su.Rproj` file at the top level.

#### Part B: Confirm the working directory

Open the R Project by clicking the `.Rproj` file (or selecting it from the top-right corner of RStudio). Then run the following code in your **Console** (not in the render!!) and paste the output below:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
getwd()
```
:::


**Paste your `getwd()` output here:**

```
# Example: "/Users/yourname/Documents/PUBH_523_26Su"
```

#### Part C: Does the working directory match?

Now use `getwd()` in a code chunk and render the document. Does the output match what you got in Part B? 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
getwd()
```
:::


::: callout-tip
##### Tip

You'll need to remove the `#| eval: false` line to run the code and display your output.
:::

#### Part C: Why not `setwd()`?

In 1-2 sentences, explain why you should not use `setwd()` in your qmd's, and what you should do instead.

*Write your answer here.*

### Question 5: Using `here()` for File Paths

#### Part A: Explore `here()`

With your R Project open, run the following in a code chunk and paste the output:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pacman::p_load(here)
here()
```
:::


**Paste the output of `here()` here:**

```         
# Example: "/Users/yourname/Documents/PUBH_523_26Su"
```

Does the output match your `getwd()` output from Question 2 Part B? It should! Briefly explain why they match (1 sentence).

*Write your answer here.*

#### Part B: Build a file path with `here()`

Imagine you saved a data file called `survey_data.csv` in your `data_practice/` folder. Write the `here()` call you would use to build the path to that file from inside a `.qmd` in your `practice/` folder:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Remove the ___ and fill in the here() call
file_path <- here("___", "___")
file_path
```
:::


**New skill: two ways to write `here()` paths**

As shown in Lesson 10, you can write the path two ways:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Option 1: comma-separated (platform-agnostic вЂ” preferred)
here("data_practice", "survey_data.csv")

# Option 2: slash-separated
here("data_practice/survey_data.csv")
```
:::


Which option do you prefer and why? (1вЂ“2 sentences)

*Write your answer here.*

#### Part C: Console vs. render

One reason we use `here()` is that it gives the same path whether you run code in the **Console** or **render** the document. In Lesson 10, we saw that working directories can differ between those two contexts.

Explain in 2вЂ“3 sentences: what problem does `here()` solve that an R Project alone does not fully fix?

*Write your answer here.*

## Submission Checklist

Before submitting, confirm each item:

- [ ] Name updated in YAML header
- [ ] `PUBH_523_26Su` (or `PUBH_623_26Su`) folder created with all subfolders
- [ ] Screenshot of folder structure inserted and visible in the rendered HTML (Q1 Part B)
- [ ] All course packages loaded successfully with `pacman::p_load()` (Q2 Part A)
- [ ] Package table filled in with category and description for each (Q2 Part B)
- [ ] `install.packages()` question answered in Q2 Part C
- [ ] `author:` field updated and date appears correctly in rendered HTML (Q3 Part A)
- [ ] Level 3 heading and paragraph added (Q3 Part B)
- [ ] Code chunk options updated to hide code and suppress warnings (Q3 Part C)
- [ ] R Project (`.Rproj`) created inside your course folder (Q4 Part A)
- [ ] `getwd()` output pasted from Console (Q4 Part B)
- [ ] `getwd()` chunk rendered and compared to Part B output (Q4 Part C)
- [ ] `setwd()` question answered (Q4 Part D)
- [ ] `here()` output pasted and explained (Q5 Part A)
- [ ] `here()` file path filled in (Q5 Part B)
- [ ] Console vs. render question answered (Q5 Part C)
- [ ] Document renders to HTML with no errors
- [ ] Files named correctly: `Lastname_Firstinitial_Practice_02.qmd` and `Lastname_Firstinitial_Practice_02.html` or `lastname_firstinitial_practice_02.qmd` and `lastname_firstinitial_practice_02.html`
- [ ] Both `.qmd` and `.html` submitted to Brightspace

## Note on AI usage

I used GenAI (Claude) to help me draft this practice assignment. It helped me brainstorm ideas for the assignment, and I directed it to help you complete the needed tasks that I showed in the lessons. 

The submission checklist was Claude's idea. I really like it! Let me know what you think!
